# Observability Standard

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every production system SHOULD be observable: it should produce enough
structured telemetry that operators can diagnose its behavior without
modifying its code. Observability is not monitoring — monitoring answers
known questions ("is the error rate above 5%?"), while observability
enables answering questions that were not anticipated at instrumentation
time.

Three pillars support observability: structured logs (discrete events),
metrics (aggregated measurements over time), and distributed traces
(request paths across service boundaries). A system that produces all
three in correlated, structured formats gives operators the ability to
move from symptom to cause without deploying new instrumentation.

## What Good Looks Like

- Systems produce structured logs with consistent fields: timestamp,
  severity, service name, trace ID, and contextual attributes. Logs are
  machine-parseable, not free-text strings.
- Metrics cover the four golden signals at minimum: latency, traffic,
  errors, and saturation. Custom business metrics supplement these where
  the domain requires them.
- Distributed traces connect request flows across service boundaries.
  Trace context propagates through all synchronous and asynchronous
  calls within the system's control.
- Dashboards exist for each service and are maintained as code or
  configuration, not as one-off manual creations that rot silently.
- Alerts are actionable. Every alert has a runbook or at minimum a
  description of what the alert means and what the responder should do.
  Alert noise is tracked and reduced — if an alert fires and requires
  no action, it is either tuned or removed.
- Unknown-unknowns are discoverable through ad-hoc exploration. Operators
  can slice and dice telemetry along arbitrary dimensions without
  pre-defining those dimensions in advance.
- Telemetry has appropriate retention policies. Recent data is high-
  resolution; historical data is downsampled, not deleted entirely.

## Gap Analysis Guide

- **No structured telemetry.** The system produces only unstructured
  logs or no output at all. Diagnosis requires reading source code.
- **Logs exist but are unstructured.** Free-text log lines that require
  regex parsing to extract meaning. Correlation across services is
  impossible.
- **Metrics exist but lack golden signals.** Custom metrics are present
  but fundamental operational signals are missing.
- **No distributed tracing.** Request flows across service boundaries
  are invisible. Latency attribution is guesswork.
- **Alerts are noise.** Alerts fire frequently, require no action, and
  train operators to ignore them. Signal is lost in noise.
- **Dashboards are stale.** Dashboards were created once and no longer
  reflect the system's current architecture or failure modes.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 chemical plant needed gauges, strip-chart recorders, and alarm
panels so operators could understand system state without opening
vessels. A 2070 distributed system needs the same visibility through
different instruments. The principle is identical: systems that cannot
be observed cannot be operated safely. The instrumentation evolves;
the need for visibility does not.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain standard extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer standards.
